zh.Stanton St John, Oxfordshire, Inglaterra: 英國英格蘭奧可福爾斯坦頓 聖 約翰
zh.lifeSummary: 當Katherine de Say於1310年出生在英國肯特時，她的父親Geoffrey de Saye29歲，母親Idoine Leybourne30歲。她和John Porter III至少有2個女兒。她去世於1355年9月29日在英國英格蘭素瑞享年45歲。

nameMeanings.name: de
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Dilip, Anirban, Gopal, Alok, Anindya, Arunava, Asit, Asok, Dibyendu, Goutam, Indra, Indranil.

variants.name: Dey
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Ashit, Indu, Naina, Partha, Soumya, Ajit, Anjan, Anjana, Anup, Arjun, Arun, Ashim.
English, Scottish, and Welsh: variant of   Day  .
German: topographic name from a field name in North Rhine-Westphalia, denoting a sizeable piece of land, from Middle Low German dē ‘thigh’ (referring to the shape of the property).

nameMeanings.name: Say
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: of Norman origin, a habitational name from Sai in Orne or Say in Indre, France, perhaps so called from a Gaulish personal name Saius + the Latin locative suffix -acum (see also French name 3 below).

English: nickname, perhaps for someone who made or sold say, a type of woollen cloth, or for an habitual wearer of clothes made of this material, from Middle English sai(e) ‘say’ (Old French saie, from Latin saga, plural of sagum ‘military cloak’). See also Sayer .

French (southern): topographic name from saix (from Latin saxum) ‘rock’, or a habitational name from a place called with this word, for example, Say in Loire, Saix in Tarn and Vienne, Le Saix in Hautes-Alpes, or Les Saix in Isère.

variants.name: Sey
meanings: English and Scottish: probably a variant of   Say   particularly prevalent in Scotland.
Dutch and Flemish: from a short form of the ancient Germanic personal name Sigiheri (see Seger ).
West African (Ghana): Akan (Fante) name of unexplained etymology.

variants.name: Sayer
meanings: English: common occupational name from Middle English sayer, a phonetic variant of Sawyer , which in Wales and neighboring English counties may have been reinforced by the synonymous Welsh saer ‘carpenter’ and everywhere by a possible borrowing into Middle English of Old French seieor, saieur ‘sawyer’.

English: rare occupational name perhaps denoting an assayer of metals, or a servant who tasted his lord's food before serving, from Middle English saiour ‘assayer, tester’ (a shortened form of Old French assaiour, ultimately from Late Latin exagium, a derivative of exagmināre ‘to weigh’).
English: rare occupational name for a professional reciter or minstrel, from Middle English seier(e) ‘speaker’.

variants.name: Saye
meanings: Americanized form of Slovenian Saje: nickname for someone with very dark, sooty hair, derived from the root of sajast ‘sooty’, or directly from saja ‘soot’.
West African (Liberia): from the personal name Saye, found among the Mano (Mah) people, denoting a first-born boy.

nameMeanings.name: Katherine
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.

historicEvents.title: Famine Strikes Europe
historicEvents.description: A famine caused by cold winters and wet summers across Europe caused millions to die due to starvation. Desperation from hunger caused high levels of crime, stampedes in bread lines in London, infanticide, and even cannibalism throughout Europe.

historicEvents.title: First War of Scottish Independence
historicEvents.description: The First War of Scottish Independence began after England invaded Scotland in 1296. The Treaty of Edinburgh was signed in 1328 which declared peace between the two kingdoms and brought the war to an end.

historicEvents.title: The Hundred Years' War
historicEvents.description: The Hundred Years' War between France and England was largely due to the question of legitimate succession to the French crown.

en.lifeSummary: When Katherine de Say was born in 1310, in Kent, England, her father, Geoffrey de Saye, was 29 and her mother, Idoine Leybourne, was 30. She had at least 2 daughters with John Porter III. She died on 29 September 1355, in Surrey, England, United Kingdom, at the age of 45.
